frameworks.name: reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: value-based learning of action policies from reward feedback

frameworks.name: episodic memory
frameworks.one_line: traces of individual events used to augment RL value estimation

frameworks.name: semantic memory
frameworks.one_line: gradual extraction of action values across many experiences

core_question: Can endowing reinforcement learning systems with episodic memory resolve computational limits that arise in high-dimensional, sparse, long-horizon real-world tasks?
key_assumptions: classical RL assumes tractable low-dimensional state spaces
real-world state spaces are high-dimensional, continuous, partially observable
memory systems can be functionally dissociated into procedural, semantic, and episodic
value functions over complex states can be approximated from individual event traces
manipulations: review-style synthesis: contrasts purely semantic-memory RL with episodic-memory-augmented RL across tasks
key_findings: episodic memory enables efficient approximation of value functions over complex state spaces
episodic traces allow learning from very sparse data, including one-shot situations
episodic retrieval helps bridge long-term temporal dependencies between actions and rewards
interpretations: the diverse roles of memory in RL are best understood as one integrated learning system rather than separate modules
episodic memory and RL jointly address both the computational and cognitive gaps in standard RL accounts
limitations_noted: standard experimental RL tasks are simple and ignore key real-world features (high-dimensional, sparse, long-horizon)
